The healthiest hub for today's political conversation

Defuse political conversations by seeing the full spectrum before you decide.

Defuse presents the best arguments from every political perspective — side by side, in digestible bite-sized pieces — so you can think for yourself.

Explore the Conversation Why it's needed
The Problem

Your algorithm is designed to piss you off.

No matter how open-minded we say we want to be, we all — consciously or not — curate our own echo chambers when we consume cultural and political opinions. We follow the voices that validate us and mute the ones that don't. It's not a character flaw — it's human nature, supercharged by technology.

2x
of Americans imagine twice as many opponents hold "extreme" views as actually do
77%
of Americans think others live in a media bubble — but only 32% think they do
0
platforms built to present every perspective fairly, side by side
How Defuse Works

Three principles. One platform.

Every issue. Every angle. Concise enough to read in the time it takes to scroll through a single news article.

Transparency

Every voice on Defuse is clearly identified on the political spectrum. No hidden agendas, no pretending to be neutral. You always know where someone stands — and you can decide for yourself.

Curated Diversity

No matter how hard you try, your information stream is not curated to take in events and issues with balance. We can do it for you.

Steel manning A "straw man" is when you deliberately weaken someone's position to defeat it easily. For example, your brother advocates for bike lanes, and you accuse him of wanting to ban cars. A steel-man does the opposite. Your brother's bike lane position? Defuse would say that it optimizes urban efficiency and safety by decongesting traffic and improving infrastructure longevity.

Our AI doesn't debate or judge — it strengthens. For every perspective, Defuse generates the most charitable interpretation, so you can engage with the best version of ideas you may otherwise disagree with.

Learn more about our methodology →
A New Consumption Model

Read smarter, not angrier.

The Old Way

  • One perspective, constantly reinforcing prior beliefs
  • Implicit bias you can't see
  • Cynical view of the other side
  • Algorithms feed your existing beliefs
  • Engagement optimized for outrage
→

The Defuse Way

  • Multiple perspectives, same page
  • Explicit bias, clearly labeled
  • Charitable view of the other side
  • Curated for intellectual diversity
  • AI-powered steel manning of every take
"A house divided against itself cannot stand."
— Abraham Lincoln

Ready to see the whole picture?

Explore how America's sharpest minds think about the issues that matter most.

The Method

How we score the political spectrum.

Every commentator on Defuse is mapped across five dimensions using a 1–10 scale.

The Five Dimensions

Domestic Policy (1–10)

1 = Market-driven. Favors deregulation, low taxes, privatization, and minimal government involvement.

10 = Heavy government involvement. Favors universal programs, public investment, strong safety nets, and an active government role.

Foreign Policy (1–10)

1 = Hawkish. Supports a strong military posture, alliances like NATO, and willingness to use force.

10 = Isolationist. Skeptical of military action, favors diplomacy, and opposes foreign entanglements.

Trade (1–10)

1 = Free trade. Supports open markets, low tariffs, and international trade agreements.

10 = Protectionist. Favors tariffs, reshoring, industrial policy, and skepticism of trade deals.

Cultural (1–10)

1 = Conservative. Emphasizes religious values, traditional institutions, and national identity.

10 = Progressive. Prioritizes social justice, secular governance, and expanding rights for marginalized groups.

Orthodoxy (1–10)

1 = Independent. Breaks with their "expected" side frequently. Holds views that don't fit neatly into left or right.

10 = Party-line. Reliably aligns with their party or ideological camp on nearly every issue.

How scores are assigned

Scores are based on each commentator's publicly stated positions — in their writing, shows, podcasts, social media, and interviews. We look at patterns, not one-off quotes. A commentator's score reflects the weight of their overall body of work, not a single hot take.

The goal is to give you a meaningful map of where people stand relative to each other across multiple dimensions, so that a libertarian looks different from a neoconservative, and a progressive looks different from a socialist. Over time, we are hopeful to use an LLM for more precise "scores" — but for now, treat these as imperfect but directionally accurate.

Political Archetypes

Spider charts visualize at a glance how someone's politics are shaped. Here are some common political archetypes and what their charts look like.

The Libertarian

Free markets, free trade, non-interventionist, culturally moderate, and deeply heterodox. Doesn't fit neatly on either team.

The Neoconservative

Market-oriented domestic policy, hawkish on foreign policy, free trade, culturally moderate-right, and fairly party-line.

The MAGA Populist

Mixed on domestic policy, non-interventionist, highly protectionist, culturally traditional, and fiercely party-line.

The Progressive

Strongly redistributive, dovish, mixed on trade, culturally progressive, and fairly party-line.

The Centrist / Pragmatist

Moderate across every dimension and highly heterodox. The chart is small and symmetrical — no strong pull in any direction.

The Left Populist

Strongly redistributive, dovish, protectionist, culturally progressive, and heterodox — breaks with the Democratic establishment.

A spider chart is a fingerprint

Each axis extends from the center (1) to the outer edge (10). The filled shape shows a commentator's profile at a glance. A large, spiky shape means someone holds strong views across multiple dimensions. A small, compact shape near the center means someone is moderate or mixed.

The most interesting shapes are the asymmetric ones — they reveal the people who defy simple categorization. A commentator who is far-right on domestic policy but progressive on culture, or hawkish on foreign policy but protectionist on trade, will have a distinctive shape that immediately tells you they're not a cookie-cutter partisan.

About

Built by the politically homeless.

Defuse was started by two people who listen to Pod Save America on a Monday and The Ben Shapiro Show on a Tuesday — not out of confusion, but out of curiosity. We wanted to hear the smartest version of every argument, not just the ones our algorithm picked for us.

We kept coming back to the same thought experiment: what would happen if you took the sharpest voices across the political spectrum and stranded them on a desert island together? No audiences to perform for. No engagement metrics. No reason to dunk on each other. Just the arguments.

We think they might actually like each other.

That's a naive thought, and we know it. But Defuse was built on the belief that naivety might be exactly what political media needs right now. The entire industry is optimized to make you angry, afraid, and certain that the other side is either stupid or evil. We think that's a design flaw, not a feature.

So we built something different. Defuse puts left, center, and right side by side — on the same issue, on the same page. Every take gets a steel man: the most charitable, intellectually honest interpretation, even for ideas you disagree with. Especially for ideas you disagree with.

We're not centrists. We're not "both sides" people. We have our own views. But we believe you can hold strong convictions and still think the other side has something worth hearing. That's not weakness. That's the whole point.

Who's included — and who's not.

We curated roughly 150 of the most influential political commentators in the United States. We think diversity of opinion is important — essential, actually. That's the whole premise of this project.

But while we believe the range of acceptable ideas should be broad, it should not be unlimited. If you think we got a commentator selection wrong, either by including someone we shouldn't have, or leaving someone out that needs to be heard, let us know. Similarly, if you are part of the commentariat and would like to push back on your political score, we want to hear about it.

We want to grow this.

Defuse is two people and an idea. We want it to be more. If you're a journalist, a developer, a designer, a political science nerd, or just someone who's tired of being told what to think — we want to hear from you.

If any part of this resonates, reach out. We're building this in the open and we want collaborators, not just users.

Get in touch →

Steel Man Central™ A "straw man" is when you deliberately weaken someone's position to defeat it easily. For example, your brother advocates for bike lanes, and you accuse him of wanting to ban cars. A steel man does the opposite. Your brother's bike lane position? Defuse would say that it optimizes urban efficiency and safety by decongesting traffic and improving infrastructure longevity.

The strongest version of every argument — left, right, and alternative. AI-generated, editorially curated, and designed to make you think harder, not angrier.

🤖 Arguments are generated and refined by AI, then editorially reviewed for accuracy and intellectual honesty. Commentator tags reflect likely agreement based on their public positions, not direct endorsement. Learn about our methodology →
